risk/basis-risk.html.title: Basis Risk
risk/basis-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Basis risk is the risk that arises from the imperfect correlation between two related instruments or markets used in hedging.Examples:
A trader hedging an interest rate exposure with a futures contract on a similar but not identical bond faces losses due to the difference in price movements between the two instruments.

risk/credit-risk.html.title: Credit Risk
risk/credit-risk.html.description:
Definition: Credit risk is the risk of financial loss due to a counterparty's failure to meet its contractual obligations, such as failing to repay a loan or deliver securities.Examples: Counterparty Risk: A bank suffers a loss when a borrower defaults on a loan. Issuer Risk: An investor holding a corporate bond experiences losses when the issuing company defaults on interest payments. Settlement Risk
Sovereign Risk
Settlement Risk
Liquidity Risk
Model Risk
Reputational Risk

risk/event-risk.html.title: Event Risk
risk/event-risk.html.description: Definition: Event risk refers to the financial impact of unexpected, significant events that disrupt markets or institutions.Examples: Natural disasters, terrorist attacks, or corporate scandals trigger sudden and severe financial losses.

risk/foreign-exchange-risk.html.title: Foreign Exchange Risk
risk/foreign-exchange-risk.html.description:
Definition:
FX risk is the risk of losses arising from unfavorable changes in currency exchange rates.Examples:
An exporter pricing goods in a foreign currency experiences reduced revenue if the foreign currency depreciates against the home currency.

risk/inflation-risk.html.title: Inflation Risk
risk/inflation-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Inflation risk is the risk of purchasing power erosion due to rising prices.Examples:
An investor holding a bond with a fixed coupon rate experiences real returns that diminish when inflation rises unexpectedly.

risk/interest-rate-risk.html.title: Interest Rate Risk
risk/interest-rate-risk.html.description: Definition: Interest rate risk arises from changes in interest rates, which can impact the value of fixed-income instruments or interest-sensitive financial products.Examples: A bank holding a portfolio of fixed-rate loans incurs losses when interest rates rise because the loans lose value.

risk/legal-risk.html.title: Legal Risk
risk/legal-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Legal risk arises from unenforceable contracts, regulatory penalties, or litigation.Examples:
A financial institution executes a trade without properly adhering to the legal documentation, resulting in disputes or losses.

risk/liquidity-risk.html.title: Liquidity Risk
risk/liquidity-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Liquidity risk is the risk of being unable to buy or sell assets quickly enough to prevent a loss or meet obligations due to inadequate market liquidity.Examples:
Market Liquidity Risk: An investor holding a large position in a thinly traded bond cannot sell it without significantly impacting its price.
Funding Liquidity Risk: A bank fails to meet its short-term obligations because it cannot raise funds quickly enough.

risk/market-risk.html.title: Market Risk
risk/market-risk.html.description:
Definition: Market risk refers to the potential for losses arising from adverse movements in market variables such as interest rates, exchange rates, equity prices, or commodity prices.Examples: Interest Rate Risk: A bondholder experiences losses when interest rates rise, as bond prices fall. Currency Risk (FX Risk): A company with revenue in a foreign currency incurs losses when the home currency strengthens against the foreign currency. Equity Risk: A portfolio of stocks decreases in value due to a market-wide downturn. Systemic Risk
Basis Risk
Political Risk
Event Risk
Interest Rate Risk
Foreign Exchange Risk
Model Risk
Inflation Risk

risk/market-risk-intro-notes.html.title: Market Risk Intro Notes
risk/market-risk-intro-notes.html.description:
What is Risk? How prices in market impact out PnL Element of uncertainty Possibility of loss that we will lose money When talk about risk, it's more about what will we lose Risk Attributes: Uncertainty Possibility of Loss Risk Management (4 key pillars) Avoidance Core function: How manage your markets, products, sectors Reduction Exposure? Sharing Retention We know we can manage it and manage it effectively Risk Vs return Higher Risk → Higher Return A loss known with certainty ≠ risk

Explore Types of Risk
Interact with them hereTip! Hover over to read more

Specific / Unsystematic Risk View a key summary on both: Systematic Risk vs Unsystematic Risk Types of Market Risk
Interest Rate Risk Equity Price Risk
Foreign Exchange Risk Commodity Price Risk

risk/model-risk.html.title: Model Risk
risk/model-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Model risk occurs when financial models used for pricing, risk assessment, or decision-making are flawed or inaccurate.Examples:
A pricing model underestimates the risk of a complex derivative, leading to unexpected losses.

risk/operational-risk.html.title: Operational Risk
risk/operational-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Operational risk refers to the risk of loss due to failures in internal processes, systems, human errors, or external events.Examples:
System Failure: A trading platform crashes during a high-volume trading session.
Human Error: A trader mistakenly enters the wrong trade order, causing financial losses.
Fraud: An employee commits unauthorized transactions, resulting in reputational and financial harm.

risk/political-risk.html.title: Political Risk
risk/political-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Political risk is the potential for financial losses due to political instability, regulatory changes, or government actions.Examples:
An investment in a foreign country loses value after a government implements nationalization or expropriation policies.

risk/regulatory-risk.html.title: Regulatory Risk
risk/regulatory-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Regulatory risk is the risk of losses or operational disruption due to changes in laws, regulations, or supervisory requirements.Examples:
A bank incurs significant costs to comply with stricter capital requirements imposed by regulators.

risk/reputational-risk.html.title: Reputational Risk
risk/reputational-risk.html.description: Definition: Reputational risk refers to the potential loss of trust or reputation that can harm a firm’s client relationships and financial standing.Examples: A bank accused of misconduct or fraud faces customer withdrawals, loss of business, and regulatory fines.

risk/risks-tree.html.title: Risks Tree
risk/risks-tree.html.description:
Financial RisksCredit Risk
Market Risk
FX RiskCommodity Price Risk
Interest Rate RiskEquity Price RiskGap RiskTrading RiskSpecific RiskGeneral Market RiskIssuer RiskCounterparty Credit RiskIssue RiskPortfolio ConcentrationTransaction Risk

risk/settlement-risk.html.title: Settlement Risk
risk/settlement-risk.html.description:
Definition:
Settlement risk is the risk that one party in a transaction delivers their obligation (e.g., securities or cash), but the counterparty fails to deliver their side of the transaction.Examples:
Herstatt Risk: A bank delivers a currency in one time zone but does not receive the counter-currency from the counterparty in another time zone due to their default.

risk/sovereign-risk.html.title: Sovereign Risk
risk/sovereign-risk.html.description: Definition: Sovereign risk refers to the risk of a government defaulting on its debt obligations or imposing capital controls that restrict payments.Examples: An investor holding bonds issued by a government faces losses when the government imposes foreign exchange restrictions or fails to make interest payments.

risk/systematic-risk-vs-unsystematic-ris.title: Systematic Risk vs Unsystematic Risk
risk/systematic-risk-vs-unsystematic-ris.description:
Quick Tip to Remember: Market = Large Specific = Small Systematic = System-Wide Unsystematic = Not the whole system... therefore specific Market risk also called systematic risk is risk that affects the entire market or a large portion of it. Source: Comes from broad economic or financial factors. Key Idea You cannot eliminate it through diversification. Interest rate changes Inflation Recessions or economic downturns Geopolitical events Market-wide sentiment shifts Example If global interest rates rise sharply, most equities and bonds may fall. Even a well-diversified portfolio would still be affected. Interest rate risk Inflation risk Currency risk Market crash risk
Specific risk also called unsystematic risk or idiosyncratic risk is risk unique to a particular company, industry, or asset (product, security, equity, etc.) Key Idea You can reduce or eliminate it by diversification. Poor company management Product failures Fraud or lawsuits Industry-specific regulation Supply chain disruptions Example If a single company’s CEO resigns unexpectedly their stock might drop sharply. But other companies may be unaffected.

risk/systemic-risk.html.title: Systemic Risk
risk/systemic-risk.html.description:
Definition:
The risk of widespread financial instability caused by the failure of a single large institution or a breakdown in a critical financial market, which can have ripple effects across the entire financial system.Examples:
The failure of a systemically important financial institution (SIFI), such as Lehman Brothers during the 2008 financial crisis.
A collapse in interbank lending markets leading to a liquidity crisis

risk/types-of-risk.html.title: Types of Risk
risk/types-of-risk.html.description:

Equity Risk: A portfolio of stocks decreases in value due to a market-wide downturn. Human Error: A trader mistakenly enters the wrong trade order, causing financial losses. Fraud: An employee commits unauthorized transactions, resulting in reputational and financial harm. Definition: Reputational risk refers to the potential loss of trust or reputation that can harm a firm’s client relationships and financial standing.Examples: A bank accused of misconduct or fraud faces customer withdrawals, loss of business, and regulatory fines. Definition: Legal risk arises from unenforceable contracts, regulatory penalties, or litigation.Examples: A financial institution executes a trade without properly adhering to the legal documentation, resulting in disputes or losses. Definition: Sovereign risk refers to the risk of a government defaulting on its debt obligations or imposing capital controls that restrict payments.Examples: An investor holding bonds issued by a government faces losses when the government imposes foreign exchange restrictions or fails to make interest payments. Definition: Inflation risk is the risk of purchasing power erosion due to rising prices.Examples: An investor holding a bond with a fixed coupon rate experiences real returns that diminish when inflation rises unexpectedly.
Definition: Event risk refers to the financial impact of unexpected, significant events that disrupt markets or institutions.Examples: Natural disasters, terrorist attacks, or corporate scandals trigger sudden and severe financial losses. risk/var.html.title: VaR
risk/var.html.description:
2025-01-09 Case Studies Notes > ^6f767c Market risk is best measured as "value at risk" using probability analysis based upon a common confidence interval (e.g., two standard deviations) and time horizon (e.g., a one-day exposure).
G30_Summary, page 7 reducing market risks across derivatives to a single common denominator makes aggregation, comparison, and risk control easier. "Value at risk" is the expected loss from an adverse market movement with a specified probability over a particular period of time. For example, with 97.5% probability (that is, a "confidence interval" of 97.5%), corresponding to calculations using about two standard deviations, it can be determined that any change in portfolio value over one day resulting from an adverse market movement will not exceed a specific amount. Conversely, there is a 2.5% probability of experiencing an adverse change in excess of the calculated amount. Value at risk should encompass changes in all major market risk components listed in the recommendation. The difficulty in applying the technique of value at risk increases with the complexity of the risks being managed. For comparability, value at risk should be calculated to a common confidence interval and time horizon
G30_Summary, page 7Unlike VaR, ES considers the severity of losses beyond the chosen confidence level, resulting in a more comprehensive risk assessment. ES emphasizes the possibility for more substantial losses during volatile periods such as the financial crisis, providing a deeper understanding of the portfolio's downside risk.

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
